//: Opt-in curated research / investigation best-practice lessons. Seed with: localpilot learning seed --file seed-packs/research-lessons.json. Original, general, model-actionable; not auto-seeded.

lessons.body: State a falsifiable hypothesis, then pick the single test that most cheaply tells it apart from the alternatives, and run that test first. Investigation is a sequence of discriminating tests, not a pile of observations.

Do not anchor on the first plausible explanation. Hold two or three hypotheses at once and design a test that discriminates them; abandon a hypothesis the moment a clean test refutes it, even one you favoured.
lessons.evidence: Distilled from an investigation where two favoured hypotheses were each refuted by a decisive test before the real cause was found.

lessons.body: Separate what you verified from what you assumed. Tag each claim with its evidence; an unverified claim is a question, not a conclusion, and should be labelled as such.
Record the exact command, environment, and raw numbers behind every measured result so it can be reproduced and re-run. A number without its setup is not evidence.
A null or negative result is a result. Record it as observed; do not re-run the experiment until it turns favourable, and do not quietly drop it.
Prefer primary and official sources over summaries when a fact matters: the spec, the API documentation, the source code, the changelog. Note the date, because facts expire.
When elimination narrows a problem, test the common factor directly. If A and B both fail and share C, test C in isolation before blaming A or B.
lessons.evidence: When two builds and two models all produced garbage, the shared factor (the runtime) was the cause.

lessons.body: Beware confirmation bias in tooling: a flag, a warning, or a search hit can mislead. Confirm the mechanism behind a signal, not just the correlated signal itself.
Adversarially verify your own conclusion: try to refute it with one more test before committing to it. The cheapest skeptic is you, before you ship the claim.
Re-validate carried-forward findings against the current state before acting on them. A known issue may already be fixed; acting blindly churns working things and wastes effort.
Scope the question before researching it. An underspecified question yields an unfocused answer; pin the constraints — version, platform, budget, goal — first.
Bisect to localize: when something broke between a known-good and a known-bad state, halve the search space repeatedly rather than guessing the cause.
